content:
The resource APIs are stable now, and half the tutorials showing them are already outdated. Here’s where httpResource actually earns its place in a production app and the three places I wouldn’t touch.

6 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last sprint, a teammate opened a PR that converted eleven HttpClient calls to httpResource. All eleven. GETs, POSTs, a file upload, everything. His reasoning was fair the API went stable in Angular 22, the tutorials make it look like the future, and nobody wants to be the person maintaining the old pattern.
I approved three of the eleven.
That review is basically this article. httpResource is genuinely good for a specific shape of problem. Used everywhere, it makes some code worse. The line between those two outcomes is clear once you've seen it, so let me draw it.
What httpResource actually is
It’s a signal wrapper around HttpClient. Not a replacement for it it literally uses HttpClient underneath, which means your interceptors, XSRF setup, and testing utilities keep working untouched. That detail matters more than it sounds: you can adopt it per-call…
